questions.question: Each factor of production earns an income. What correctly identifies the income for labour and capital? labour               capital A profit               interest, B rent                  profit, C wages                profit, D wages              interest
questions.text: Each factor of production earns an income. What correctly identifies the income for labour and capital? labour               capital A profit               interest, B rent                  profit, C wages                profit, D wages              interest
options: A profit               interest
B rent                  profit
C wages                profit
D wages              interest

questions.question: What could be the opportunity cost of a nuclear power station? A the running costs of the power station, B a coal-fired power station, C the current value of the power station, D the cost of building the power station
questions.text: What could be the opportunity cost of a nuclear power station? A the running costs of the power station, B a coal-fired power station, C the current value of the power station, D the cost of building the power station
options: A the running costs of the power station
B a coal-fired power station
C the current value of the power station
D the cost of building the power station

questions.question: The diagram shows a production possibility curve for maize and cotton. maize cotton O Q S P R Bad weather causes a poor harvest for both crops. Which movement could be used to represent this change? A P to R B Q to R C S to Q D S to R
questions.text: The diagram shows a production possibility curve for maize and cotton. maize cotton O Q S P R Bad weather causes a poor harvest for both crops. Which movement could be used to represent this change? A P to R B Q to R C S to Q D S to R
options: A P to R B Q to R C S to Q D S to R

questions.question: The market for a good was in equilibrium. A change occurred which resulted in a new equilibrium with a higher price for the good and a lower quantity traded. What change would have caused this? A the demand curve moved to the left, B the demand curve moved to the right, C the supply curve moved to the left, D the supply curve moved to the right 3
questions.text: The market for a good was in equilibrium. A change occurred which resulted in a new equilibrium with a higher price for the good and a lower quantity traded. What change would have caused this? A the demand curve moved to the left, B the demand curve moved to the right, C the supply curve moved to the left, D the supply curve moved to the right 3
options: A the demand curve moved to the left
B the demand curve moved to the right
C the supply curve moved to the left
D the supply curve moved to the right 3

questions.question: A government subsidises the production of pineapples. This is likely to A increase the price of pineapples, B raise the costs of supplying pineapples, C raise revenue for the government, D cause the supply of pineapples to increase at every price
questions.text: A government subsidises the production of pineapples. This is likely to A increase the price of pineapples, B raise the costs of supplying pineapples, C raise revenue for the government, D cause the supply of pineapples to increase at every price
options: A increase the price of pineapples
B raise the costs of supplying pineapples
C raise revenue for the government
D cause the supply of pineapples to increase at every price

questions.question: What indicates the existence of external costs in an economy? A An international trade deficit has caused the country to be in debt., B National companies have borrowed from foreign investors., C Private costs of production are less than social costs., D Private costs of production are more than social benefits.
questions.text: What indicates the existence of external costs in an economy? A An international trade deficit has caused the country to be in debt., B National companies have borrowed from foreign investors., C Private costs of production are less than social costs., D Private costs of production are more than social benefits.

B National companies have borrowed from foreign investors.
C Private costs of production are less than social costs.
D Private costs of production are more than social benefits.

questions.question: What might be a disadvantage to a trade union when arguing for an increase in its members’ pay? A an increase in imports of a cheaper, similar product, B the closure of a local training college resulting in fewer potential workers, C the development of a new and profitable brand of the company’s product, D the development of new techniques that increase productivity

A an increase in imports of a cheaper, similar product, B the closure of a local training college resulting in fewer potential workers, C the development of a new and profitable brand of the company’s product, D the development of new techniques that increase productivity
options: A an increase in imports of a cheaper, similar product
B the closure of a local training college resulting in fewer potential workers

questions.question: What shows the correct examples of income and wealth? income                  wealth A dividends from shares wages, B rent from a house overtime payments, C overtime payments wages, D wages                             house 4

questions.question: Nazmin Uddin works in a local office as a secretary three days a week. She lives with her parents in a small apartment. Meer  Ali  owns  a  successful  fitness  centre  that  employs  about  twenty  people.  He  owns  a  large house. Which is most likely to be correct? A Nazmin spends a smaller proportion of her income than Meer., B Nazmin saves most of her income., C Nazmin would find it easier than Meer to borrow money., D Nazmin has a lower disposable income than Meer.
questions.text: Nazmin Uddin works in a local office as a secretary three days a week. She lives with her parents in a small apartment. Meer  Ali  owns  a  successful  fitness  centre  that  employs  about  twenty  people.  He  owns  a  large house. Which is most likely to be correct? A Nazmin spends a smaller proportion of her income than Meer., B Nazmin saves most of her income., C Nazmin would find it easier than Meer to borrow money., D Nazmin has a lower disposable income than Meer.
options: A Nazmin spends a smaller proportion of her income than Meer.
B Nazmin saves most of her income.
C Nazmin would find it easier than Meer to borrow money.
D Nazmin has a lower disposable income than Meer.

questions.question: A  survey  of  managers  in  the  USA  revealed  that  most  businessmen  feel  that  a  company’s responsibility is ‘to serve the interests of owners, employees, customers and the public’. The  idea  of  profit  maximisation,  in  contrast,  implies  that  a  company’s  main  responsibility  should be to the A customers, B employees, C owners, D public 5

questions.question: Some agricultural co-operatives have changed from labour-intensive to capital-intensive methods of production. What might be a cause of this change? A Average productivity of agricultural workers has increased., B Farming equipment has become more efficient., C People are eating less food for health reasons., D Some agricultural land has been sold for housing.

B Farming equipment has become more efficient.

D Some agricultural land has been sold for housing.

questions.question: A firm has fixed costs of $200 for its daily output. The table shows its daily total variable costs. output            (units)               1            2            3            4 total variable costs ($)300 400 700 800 What can be concluded about the firm’s average total cost? A it falls continuously, B it is highest at output 4 units, C it is lowest at output 4 units, D it rises continuously

questions.question: The table shows the growth rate of output (%) in four sectors of an economy. construction sector % energy sector % manufacturing sector % services sector % first quarter of 2014 0.9 0.1 –0.7 –3.4 second quarter of 2014 0.5 1.6 –0.5   0.5 Which two sectors showed an increase in the growth rate in the first half of 2014? questions.question: A government decides to sell the monopoly supply of gas to households to a private firm. How  could  the  government  protect  the  interests  of  households  after  selling  a  large  company  to the private sector? A by excluding all competition from foreign companies, B by imposing a tax on excess profits, C by introducing a quota system for imports of gas, D by removing price controls on gas
questions.text: A government decides to sell the monopoly supply of gas to households to a private firm. How  could  the  government  protect  the  interests  of  households  after  selling  a  large  company  to the private sector? A by excluding all competition from foreign companies, B by imposing a tax on excess profits, C by introducing a quota system for imports of gas, D by removing price controls on gas
options: A by excluding all competition from foreign companies
B by imposing a tax on excess profits
C by introducing a quota system for imports of gas
D by removing price controls on gas

questions.question: In  2015,  70 000  extra  people  lost  their  jobs  but  total  employment  rose  by  75 000.  What  could explain this? A Some people may have emigrated., B Some people may have gone back to education., C Some people may have re-entered the labour force., D Some people may have retired early.
questions.text: In  2015,  70 000  extra  people  lost  their  jobs  but  total  employment  rose  by  75 000.  What  could explain this? A Some people may have emigrated., B Some people may have gone back to education., C Some people may have re-entered the labour force., D Some people may have retired early.
options: A Some people may have emigrated.
B Some people may have gone back to education.
C Some people may have re-entered the labour force.
D Some people may have retired early.

questions.question: Why is nominal Gross Domestic Product not a good measure to compare the standard of living in a country between two years? A It does not account for inflation., B It does not allow for savings., C It does not include government expenditure., D It only compares export prices with import prices. 8
questions.text: Why is nominal Gross Domestic Product not a good measure to compare the standard of living in a country between two years? A It does not account for inflation., B It does not allow for savings., C It does not include government expenditure., D It only compares export prices with import prices. 8
options: A It does not account for inflation.
B It does not allow for savings.
C It does not include government expenditure.
D It only compares export prices with import prices. 8

questions.question: In 2010 there was an increase in net emigration from Ireland. What is most likely to have caused this increase? A a decrease in job security abroad, B a decrease in wage rates abroad, C an increase in Ireland’s real GDP, D an increase in unemployment in Ireland
questions.text: In 2010 there was an increase in net emigration from Ireland. What is most likely to have caused this increase? A a decrease in job security abroad, B a decrease in wage rates abroad, C an increase in Ireland’s real GDP, D an increase in unemployment in Ireland
options: A a decrease in job security abroad
B a decrease in wage rates abroad
C an increase in Ireland’s real GDP
D an increase in unemployment in Ireland

questions.question: Some goods take a greater percentage of a typical household’s total spending than others. How is this accounted for in the construction of a Retail Price Index? A by deducting the goods and services, B by giving a weight to the goods, C by taking an average of price fluctuations during a year, D by using the price elasticity of demand for the goods

B by giving a weight to the goods
C by taking an average of price fluctuations during a year
D by using the price elasticity of demand for the goods

questions.question: Which outcome is least likely to be found with greater economic development? A A greater percentage of the population are highly educated., B A greater percentage of the population are homeowners., C A greater percentage of the population are old., D A greater percentage of the population are very poor.

B A greater percentage of the population are homeowners.
C A greater percentage of the population are old.
D A greater percentage of the population are very poor.

questions.question: The chart shows total world carbon emissions in 1995 and predicted emissions by 2035. US 22% W. Europe 17% W. Europe 12% E. Europe 27% E. Europe 19% China 11% China 17% other Asia 6% other Asia 14% Middle East 3% Middle East 5% Africa 3% Africa 8% Japan 7% 19952035 developing world 27% developing world 50% developed world 50% developed world 73% Latin America 4% US 15% Latin America 6% Japan 4% If the chart proves to be correct, what will have happened by 2035? A Africa and the Middle East will have responsibility for the same amount of carbon emissions in 2035 as in 1995., B Between 1995 and 2035, the Middle East will have increased its industrialisation by as much as Latin America., C By 2035 the combined percentage of carbon emissions caused by Africa and Latin America will have doubled., D The  US  will  have  decreased  its  carbon  emissions  by  a  smaller  percentage  than  Western Europe.
questions.text: The chart shows total world carbon emissions in 1995 and predicted emissions by 2035. US 22% W. Europe 17% W. Europe 12% E. Europe 27% E. Europe 19% China 11% China 17% other Asia 6% other Asia 14% Middle East 3% Middle East 5% Africa 3% Africa 8% Japan 7% 19952035 developing world 27% developing world 50% developed world 50% developed world 73% Latin America 4% US 15% Latin America 6% Japan 4% If the chart proves to be correct, what will have happened by 2035? A Africa and the Middle East will have responsibility for the same amount of carbon emissions in 2035 as in 1995., B Between 1995 and 2035, the Middle East will have increased its industrialisation by as much as Latin America., C By 2035 the combined percentage of carbon emissions caused by Africa and Latin America will have doubled., D The  US  will  have  decreased  its  carbon  emissions  by  a  smaller  percentage  than  Western Europe.
options: A Africa and the Middle East will have responsibility for the same amount of carbon emissions in 2035 as in 1995.
B Between 1995 and 2035, the Middle East will have increased its industrialisation by as much as Latin America.
C By 2035 the combined percentage of carbon emissions caused by Africa and Latin America will have doubled.
D The  US  will  have  decreased  its  carbon  emissions  by  a  smaller  percentage  than  Western Europe.

questions.question: A Japanese car manufacturer decided to produce its cars in a factory in Europe. What would not be a reason why they might have chosen to do this? A cheaper wage costs in Europe, B the availability of raw materials, C to gain external economies from skilled labour in Europe, D to increase Japanese self-sufficiency
questions.text: A Japanese car manufacturer decided to produce its cars in a factory in Europe. What would not be a reason why they might have chosen to do this? A cheaper wage costs in Europe, B the availability of raw materials, C to gain external economies from skilled labour in Europe, D to increase Japanese self-sufficiency
options: A cheaper wage costs in Europe
B the availability of raw materials
C to gain external economies from skilled labour in Europe
D to increase Japanese self-sufficiency

questions.question: A government removed the quota on goods imported into the country. What is the most likely result of this? A a decrease in demand for domestic production, B a decrease in domestic unemployment, C a decrease in exports, D a decrease in the balance of trade deficit
questions.text: A government removed the quota on goods imported into the country. What is the most likely result of this? A a decrease in demand for domestic production, B a decrease in domestic unemployment, C a decrease in exports, D a decrease in the balance of trade deficit
options: A a decrease in demand for domestic production
B a decrease in domestic unemployment
C a decrease in exports
D a decrease in the balance of trade deficit
